patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a cooperative, clear-speaking 19-year-old college student/barista. Start with: “I’ve been feeling worn out all the time and I look kind of yellow lately.” Early in the interview, volunteer: fatigue and getting out of breath on stairs, friends noticing yellow eyes/skin, and that your nails have long been ridged and a bit spoon-shaped.

If asked, add: occasional fast heartbeat with walking/stress, headaches/lightheadedness on standing, intermittent tea-colored urine (worse when you feel sick), left-sided belly fullness, and history of being told you might have gallstones.

Do NOT volunteer unless specifically asked about past history: anemia since childhood, being smaller/less athletic than siblings, occasional bone aches after illness, and repeated jaundice after colds/stomach bugs.

Only mention heavy, prolonged periods if directly asked about menstruation/bleeding.

Bring up a misconception: you worry you might have a bleeding disorder because you bruise easily, get nosebleeds, and once bled a lot after a dental extraction.

Express cost concerns about repeated labs, specialists, genetic testing, and missing work/school for transfusions. If the clinician shows empathy, you relax, thank them, and ask about the minimum necessary testing and affordable options.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct recognition and documentation of congenital dyserythropoietic anemia as the primary diagnosis.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as congenital dyserythropoietic anemia.
Explains in plain language that this is a rare inherited (genetic) disorder causing ineffective red blood cell production and chronic anemia.
Includes genetic testing as part of confirming/characterizing the diagnosis (e.g., to identify the subtype and support family counseling).

Mentions key supportive diagnostic features consistent with CDA (lifelong anemia, low/normal reticulocyte response for degree of anemia, indirect hyperbilirubinemia/jaundice, splenomegaly, characteristic marrow morphology) without inventing conflicting findings.
Addresses complications to consider in CDA, including iron overload (even without transfusions), gallstones, and splenomegaly, and recommends appropriate monitoring.
Avoids inappropriate first-line claims (e.g., does not present CDA as an acquired anemia; does not treat empirically with iron unless iron deficiency is demonstrated).
If any prescribing is proposed, performs an allergy_check before prescribing (critical safety check).
If any prescribing is proposed, considers drug_interaction_check and contraindication_check before prescribing (non-critical safety checks).
Recommends hematology involvement and/or referral for confirmation, subtype workup, and longitudinal management, even if not required as an outcome tool action for L1.
Communicates uncertainty appropriately if data are incomplete and frames CDA as a working diagnosis pending confirmatory testing rather than a definitive diagnosis without support.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 71-year-old retired auto body shop owner. You come in saying: “I’ve been getting really tired, and my vision gets weird and blurry sometimes.” Be vague and a bit evasive; give short answers unless asked directly.

Volunteer only: months of feeling wiped out even after sleep; on-and-off blurry vision (worse when tired or after a hot shower); pressure-like headaches that come and go; more short of breath climbing one flight of stairs.

If asked, admit: longer nosebleeds, gums bleed when brushing, tingling/numb feet at night, ringing/whooshing in ears, lightheaded standing, weight loss/low appetite, night sweats, getting full quickly.

Do not mention unless specifically probed: cold/pale/blue painful fingers/toes in cold, occasional confusion during worst episodes, foamy urine/ankle swelling, easy new bruises, mild left upper belly discomfort.

Resist discussing erectile issues, blood after bowel movements, and increased alcohol to sleep; minimize at first.

Bring up misconceptions: worry about vascular Ehlers-Danlos, Takayasu, tick-brain infection, rare sugar problem.

Push back on labs/follow-ups as “money grabs,” fear daily ibrutinib will ruin insurance, and distrust assistance programs. If clinician shows empathy, soften slightly but stay cautious about costs.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification/recording of pituitary dermoid and/or epidermoid cysts as the working diagnosis, enabling appropriate counseling and next-step planning.

Assesses for red-flag symptoms requiring urgent escalation: acute severe headache, sudden vision loss/diplopia, altered mental status, meningismus, fever, or rapidly progressive neurologic deficits.
Asks about visual symptoms (bitemporal hemianopsia, decreased acuity), headaches, and cranial nerve symptoms; documents whether symptoms are stable or progressive.
Screens for symptoms of hormonal deficiency/excess: fatigue, weight change, cold intolerance, libido/erectile dysfunction, amenorrhea/galactorrhea, polyuria/polydipsia, and growth/skin changes.
Acknowledges that definitive characterization relies on MRI features and specialist evaluation; avoids overpromising certainty without imaging correlation.

Communicates a clear treatment overview that includes surgery as the definitive management when symptomatic/enlarging and mentions steroids as a possible perioperative/indication-based therapy (e.g., perioperative stress-dose steroids or if adrenal insufficiency suspected), without implying routine outpatient steroid use.
If any medication is discussed or proposed, performs required safety checks before prescribing: confirms allergies and checks contraindications; optionally checks drug interactions (non-critical).

Avoids unsafe advice such as starting chronic steroids without evaluation, delaying urgent evaluation in the presence of red flags, or attributing symptoms to benign causes without considering pituitary apoplexy or other emergent etiologies.

nl_assertions: Elicits and documents key cluster headache features: strictly unilateral severe orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain, short duration (15–180 minutes), attack frequency (up to 8/day), circadian/cluster periodicity, and ipsilateral cranial autonomic symptoms (lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, ptosis/miosis) and/or restlessness.

Assesses red flags and secondary headache concerns (e.g., first/worst headache, neurologic deficits, fever/meningismus, new onset >50, immunosuppression, cancer history, pregnancy/postpartum, trauma) and states when urgent evaluation/imaging is needed.

Explicitly explains the diagnosis using the term 'cluster headache' and includes discussion of common triggers (at minimum alcohol during a cluster period; also sleep disruption, nitroglycerin/vasodilators, strong odors), and acknowledges familial predisposition without overstating genetics.

Explains treatment including both 'oxygen' and a 'triptan' as first-line acute options, with clear instructions on timing (at onset), administration method (non-rebreather mask; SC or intranasal triptan), expected benefit, and practical use considerations.

Performs required safety checks before prescribing: asks about allergies (especially to triptans/medications), checks contraindications to triptans (coronary artery disease, prior MI/stroke/TIA, peripheral vascular disease, uncontrolled hypertension, hemiplegic/basilar migraine, severe hepatic impairment as applicable), and screens for drug interactions (ergot derivatives within 24 hours, other triptans within 24 hours; serotonergic agents with counseling on serotonin syndrome risk; MAO inhibitors relevant to certain triptans).

Avoids unsafe or non-evidence-based acute therapy for cluster headache (e.g., routine opioids, oral triptans as primary acute therapy due to slow onset) and provides appropriate counseling on limiting medication overuse.

Addresses patient safety and follow-up: provides return precautions (new neurologic symptoms, atypical pattern, escalating severity, chest pain after triptan, shortness of breath) and plans reassessment of response and need for preventive therapy (e.g., verapamil) even if not prescribed in this L2 task.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L0 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and recording of influenza as the working diagnosis.

nl_assertions: States a clear working diagnosis of influenza and explains it to the patient using the term "influenza" (not only "flu").

Elicits key symptoms and timing consistent with influenza (acute onset, fever, myalgias, headache, cough/sore throat, fatigue) and documents/reflects symptom duration to support diagnostic confidence.

Assesses red flags and severity (dyspnea, chest pain, altered mental status, dehydration/inability to keep fluids down, persistent high fever, severe weakness) and provides appropriate escalation advice if present.

Assesses risk factors for influenza complications (pregnancy, age <5 or ≥65, chronic cardiopulmonary disease, immunocompromise, diabetes, renal disease, neurologic disease) and uses these to guide management discussion.

Explains treatment options and includes "oseltamivir" in the treatment explanation, clarifying that antivirals are most effective when started within 48 hours and are especially recommended for high-risk patients or severe/progressive illness.

Does not prescribe any medication without first completing critical safety checks: confirms allergies before prescribing (allergy_check) and evaluates contraindications before prescribing (contraindication_check).
If prescribing is discussed, also considers potential drug interactions (drug_interaction_check), acknowledging it is recommended though not critical for passing.
Provides supportive care guidance (hydration, rest, antipyretics/analgesics with safe dosing principles) and avoidance of aspirin in children/adolescents.

Gives infection-control and return-to-work/school guidance (masking/hand hygiene, staying home until fever-free for at least 24 hours without antipyretics) and discusses transmission to household contacts.
Creates a clear follow-up and return-precaution plan (worsening symptoms, new dyspnea, persistent fever, signs of secondary bacterial infection, inability to hydrate).

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of trichohepatoenteric syndrome as the working diagnosis to enable appropriate counseling and next-step confirmation (genetic testing) and supportive care planning.

nl_assertions: States trichohepatoenteric syndrome (or trichohepatoenteric syndrome/THES) as the primary diagnosis and frames it as a rare genetic disorder.

Explains diagnosis in patient/caregiver-friendly language and explicitly mentions that confirmation involves genetic testing (e.g., SKIV2L/TTC37 or equivalent gene panel), fulfilling the communication milestone.

Elicits key history elements consistent with THES: early-onset/intractable diarrhea beginning in infancy, failure to thrive/poor weight gain, hair abnormalities (woolly/brittle/sparse hair), and signs of liver disease (hepatomegaly, cholestasis, jaundice) and/or recurrent infections.

Performs or verbalizes a focused exam/assessment relevant to THES (growth parameters, hydration status, dysmorphic features, hair texture, abdominal exam for hepatomegaly) and assesses severity/red flags (dehydration, shock, severe malnutrition).

Documents an appropriate differential diagnosis for chronic infantile diarrhea/failure to thrive when uncertainty exists (e.g., congenital diarrheal disorders, IBD, cystic fibrosis, celiac disease, immunodeficiency), but does not let the differential delay recording THES when clinical picture is classic.
Avoids inappropriate definitive claims if data are incomplete (e.g., does not state certainty without supportive clinical features) and appropriately uses probabilistic language when warranted.

Does not prescribe medications in this L1 task; if the agent discusses potential treatments, keeps them non-prescriptive and emphasizes supportive management and specialist involvement outside the required tool outcomes.

If any prescribing is contemplated in narrative, performs required safety checks conceptually: asks about allergies before prescribing (critical) and considers contraindications before prescribing (non-critical).

minimal_paths.description: Classic clinical picture of THES leading to recording the diagnosis and counseling about genetic confirmation.
must_collect: onset of chronic/intractable diarrhea in infancy
features of liver involvement (jaundice/cholestasis/hepatomegaly) or history of abnormal liver tests
steps: Assess chronicity and severity of diarrhea; screen for dehydration and growth faltering.
Elicit key syndromic features: hair changes, facial/dysmorphic features, recurrent infections, liver-related symptoms.
Explain that the constellation suggests trichohepatoenteric syndrome and that it is a rare genetic condition.
Discuss confirmatory genetic testing as the next step and the rationale (to confirm diagnosis and guide counseling).
Record diagnosis: Trichohepatoenteric syndrome (THES).

minimal_paths.description: Incomplete or evolving presentation; agent records THES as likely while acknowledging differential and need for genetic confirmation.
must_collect: at least one supportive feature (hair abnormality OR liver involvement OR recurrent infections)
steps: Gather core GI and growth history and identify at least one extra-intestinal supportive feature.
State working diagnosis is trichohepatoenteric syndrome (probable) and briefly note key alternatives.
Explain diagnosis and explicitly mention genetic testing to confirm.
Record diagnosis: Trichohepatoenteric syndrome (THES).

acceptable_variants.description: Agent records THES and provides a more extensive explanation including inheritance and counseling while still meeting the required milestone.
steps: Identify THES as the most likely diagnosis based on diarrhea, growth failure, and syndromic features.
Explain THES as a rare inherited condition and mention genetic testing for confirmation and family counseling.
Record diagnosis: Trichohepatoenteric syndrome (THES).

acceptable_variants.description: Agent first documents a short differential verbally, then converges on THES and records it; no additional WRITE tools are used.
steps: Briefly consider congenital diarrheal disorders, immunodeficiency, cystic fibrosis, and celiac disease.
Highlight features that point toward THES (hair and liver involvement with early-onset severe diarrhea).
Explain diagnosis and the need for genetic testing.
Record diagnosis: Trichohepatoenteric syndrome (THES).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose suspected/confirmed RCVS, start calcium channel blocker after safety checks, and make urgent neurology referral with trigger avoidance counseling.
must_collect: thunderclap headache characteristics (sudden onset, maximal at onset, recurrent episodes)
neurologic symptoms (focal deficits, seizures, visual changes)
exposures to vasoactive substances/medications (triptans, SSRIs/SNRIs, decongestants, stimulants, cannabis/cocaine/amphetamines)
past medical history relevant to contraindications (hypotension, bradycardia/heart block, heart failure)
current medication list for interaction screening
steps: Explain suspected diagnosis of reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome and relationship to thunderclap headache; set expectations about reversibility and risk of complications.
Counsel trigger avoidance and stop/avoid vasoactive agents and illicit stimulants; provide clear return precautions for neurologic deficits or worsening symptoms.
Complete allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check prior to prescribing.
Prescribe a calcium channel blocker (e.g., nimodipine or verapamil) tailored to patient factors; advise monitoring for hypotension/bradycardia and follow-up.
Refer urgently to neurology/stroke specialist for confirmation, monitoring, and imaging follow-up to document reversibility.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent records a working diagnosis of suspected RCVS while explicitly acknowledging the need to exclude aneurysmal SAH and other thunderclap etiologies, then proceeds with CCB after safety checks and urgent neurology referral.
steps: Record diagnosis as suspected RCVS (working diagnosis) and communicate that thunderclap headache requires exclusion of other emergent causes.
Complete allergy, interaction, and contraindication checks.
Prescribe calcium channel blocker with appropriate cautions and monitoring advice.
Refer urgently to neurology/stroke specialist for definitive evaluation and follow-up imaging; reinforce trigger avoidance.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent includes additional documentation of differential diagnoses before finalizing RCVS management, without changing required L3 outcomes.
steps: Record differential (e.g., aneurysmal SAH, cervical artery dissection, cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, primary angiitis of CNS, pituitary apoplexy, intracerebral hemorrhage).
Record diagnosis of RCVS as most likely based on presentation and context.
Complete allergy, interaction, and contraindication checks.
Prescribe calcium channel blocker and provide trigger avoidance counseling.
Urgent neurology referral for confirmation, complication surveillance, and imaging follow-up.
